passages.text: From Pixels to Precision: Generative Artificial Intelligence as a Paradigm Shift in Spine Imaging—Technical Foundations, Clinical Applications, and the Path to Safe Clinical Deployment

Spine imaging represents a complex diagnostic frontier characterized by anatomical variability, motion artifacts, metallic instrumentation interference, and significant inter-reader diagnostic variability (κ=0.20 across institutions). While conventional discriminative artificial intelligence (AI) models achieve >95% accuracy in detecting degenerative changes, they remain limited by data scarcity, heterogeneous protocols, and poor generalizability. In the spine, these limitations are particularly relevant because clinical decisions can often depend on subtle distinctions (such as differentiating levels of canal or foraminal stenosis, characterizing Modic endplate changes, or assessing pedicle and vertebral morphology), where small inconsistencies can meaningfully alter management or surgical planning. Generative AI (GenAI) systems—including generative adversarial networks (GANs), diffusion models, and vision-language models (VLMs)—offer a paradigm shift by learning underlying data structures to generate high-quality synthetic outputs rather than merely classifying existing data. This narrative review, conducted using SANRA (scale for the assessment of narrative review articles) methodology across PubMed, Scopus, Embase, and Cochrane Library, examined GenAI applications in spine imaging. Eligible studies included observational designs through randomized controlled trials exploring image reconstruction, synthetic computed tomography (CT) generation, segmentation, and surgical planning applications. GAN-generated synthetic magnetic resonance imaging sequences reduce scan times by ~40% while maintaining diagnostic confidence; diffusion models enable radiation-free synthetic CT for preoperative planning; and VLMs generate structured radiology reports with hallucination rates <1.12%. However, critical barriers impede clinical translation: external validation gaps reveal AI performance collapse in real-world cohorts (sensitivity drops to 54.9% in cervical fracture detection); hallucinations and anatomical inaccuracies risk misguiding implant sizing; bias amplification magnifies demographic underrepresentation; and fragmented, small datasets lack standardized benchmarks. Technical fragility, computational demands, clinician trust deficits, and unresolved regulatory frameworks for iteratively-updating systems remain unaddressed. Successful integration requires coordinated development across 5 priorities: (1) multi-institutional datasets with cross-vendor harmonization, (2) federated learning frameworks preserving privacy, (3) uncertainty quantification and explainability tools, (4) outcome-linked clinical validation replacing technical metrics, and (5) workflow-integrated systems with DICOM-native interfaces and provenance tracking.
INTRODUCTION

There is a growing role for artificial intelligence (AI) in medicine, with radiology being among the most obvious and visibly transformed fields. Over the past decade, AI models have advanced dramatically, reimagining the potential to improve diagnoses, treatment and patient outcomes. Within this broader context, spine imaging represents an especially compelling domain for these innovations, an ageing population contributing to its high diagnostic volume and clinical impact, alongside the substantial variability in anatomy, the subtle nature of spine pathology and artifacts from motion or metallic instrumentation.

Indeed, conventional imaging remains prone to interpretative inconsistency, with the shocking results of Herzog et al. reporting that one patient scanned at 10 different magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) centres had an inter-rater reliability as low as k=0.20 across centres, highlighting the significant extent of diagnostic variability. These issues have only been further exacerbated by expanding treatment options further increasing scan demand, while the shift towards minimally invasive and personalised approaches requires richer image-derived data than traditional processing pipelines can deliver. It is this interaction of complex factors, coupled with nonstandardised imaging protocols and the sheer volume of information now available from electronic health records, that have historically made spinal imaging such a difficult target for algorithmic analysis.

However, AI has the potential to address these barriers. As highlighted by Zheng et al. and Jamaludin et al., deep-learning systems can now achieve expert-level accuracy in vertebral segmentation, disc grading, spinal curvature quantification and pathology detection. Specific clinical applications of AI in spinal imaging already include osteoporosis screening, diagnosis of degenerative spine diseases, and differentiation between tuberculous and pyogenic spondylitis, as well as assistance with preoperative measurements and surgical planning.

While conventional AI models such as convolutional neural networks have achieved over 95% accuracy in detecting degenerative changes, their effectiveness remains limited by data scarcity, heterogeneous protocols and a lack of generalizability. This, combined with the high clinical demand and data complexity, has created an urgent need for AI systems that can generate high-quality information from imperfect or incomplete data.

This is where generative AI (GenAI) represents a paradigm shift. Unlike traditional discriminative models that classify or label existing data, GenAI systems, such as generative adversarial networks (GANs), diffusion models and large vision-language models (VLMs), can learn the underlying structure of imaging data and create new, realistic outputs. These models can reconstruct degraded MRI scans, synthesize computed tomography (CT) images from MRI to reduce patient radiation exposure or even generate structured patient-facing radiology reports and answer patient FAQs (frequently asked questions).

This shift can be particularly transformative in spine imaging, with recent work demonstrating the potential to alleviate key limitations in spinal imaging, where anatomical complexity, motion artifacts, metallic instrumentation and heterogenous acquisition protocols have historically limited both interpretive accuracy and data consistency. By addressing data scarcity, enhancing structural detail, reducing artifacts, reconstructing degraded inputs and enhancing workflow efficiency, generative approaches have begun to address some of the most persistent technical and clinical limitations in the field. As these models integrate multimodal data, including combinations of imaging, clinical notes and surgical metrics, they could move spine imaging closer to a more personalised and predictive model of care.

Here, we will explore how generative AI is being applied in spinal imaging, summarizing its technical foundations, clinical applications and future directions, while also examining the roadmap to proper integration of these tools into clinical practice, in order to provide optimal spine patient care.
METHODOLOGY

This narrative review was conducted following the scale for the assessment of narrative review articles (SANRA) to examine the role of generative AI in spine imaging. The detailed search string enabled a comprehensive and targeted literature review to be conducted.

The inclusion criteria involved diverse study designs including observational studies, cohort studies, case-control, randomized controlled, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses. Studies that explored the use of generative AI methods in any aspect of spine imaging, such as image reconstruction, synthetic CT generation, augmentation, segmentation, deformity analysis, cross-modality translation or multimodal reporting were eligible for inclusion. Only articles published in English were included, from inception to 2025.

The literature search was conducted across PubMed/MEDLINE, Scopus, Embase, and the Cochrane Library. The search employed a combination of MeSH (medical subject headings) and free terms such as ‘artificial intelligence,’ ‘generative AI,’ ‘GAN,’ ‘diffusion model,’ ‘synthetic CT,’ ‘synthetic MRI,’ ‘spine imaging,’ ‘vertebral segmentation,’ ‘spinal deformity,’ ‘image reconstruction,’ and ‘surgical planning.’ This approach ensured that the literature search targeted our specific area of interest.

To improve comprehensiveness, a manual search of the reference listed key papers and recently published reviews was performed. Studies that lacked primary data, standalone abstracts, conference proceedings, and unpublished or preprint manuscripts were excluded from the review. We did review preprint papers manually, if we felt they provided some significant insight into the future of generative AI. A summary of the methodology is provided in Table 1.

Searches retrieved 342 records (PubMed n=71, Ovid Embase n=100, Cochrane n=5, Scopus n=166). After deduplication, 201 unique records remained. Across the 201 included studies, applications were distributed as follows: data augmentation (n=6), image reconstruction/enhancement (n=23), cross-modality synthesis (MRI↔CT; n=41), segmentation/deformity assessment (n=52), reporting/VLMs/workflow (n=3), navigation/surgical planning/digital twin (n=8), and general or multiapplication studies (n=68), as summarized in Supplementary Tables 1 and 2.

In total, 82 studies were included in the final narrative synthesis. Across the 82 included references, papers most commonly related to foundational/validation/governance topics (n=31), cross-modality synthesis (n=13), navigation/surgical planning/digital twin (n=11), reconstruction/enhancement (n=10), augmentation/synthetic data (n=8), reporting/VLM workflows (n=5), and segmentation/deformity assessment (n=4), as summarized in Supplementary Table 3. We also manually included 3 recent preprints which were discussed in a forward-looking context but were not counted in the main included-study total.
GENERATIVE AI: A BRIEF TECHNICAL FOUNDATION

Before exploring the specific applications of generative AI in spine imaging, and the challenges and potential solutions to integrate these tools into the clinical pipeline, it is helpful to outline the core concepts underlying this emerging field. The following section summarizes the principal generative frameworks and highlights how each contributes to improving diagnostic quality, image reconstruction and clinical workflow within spinal imaging. A summary of the various generative model classes is provided in Table 2.
1. Generative Adversarial Networks

GANs are the most established class of generative models in medical imaging, primarily used for data augmentation, superresolution and artifact removal. These networks operate through the competition of 2 neural networks: a generator, which produces synthetic data, and a discriminator, which acts to distinguish them from real data. The discriminator forces the generator to iteratively improve until its outputs become indistinguishable from authentic images, providing feedback on how convincing the generator’s output is. These GANs train through a zero-sum game, where the generator wants to fool the discriminator and thus must become better at producing realistic data, and the discriminator wants to catch the generator and hence must become better at identifying subtle flaws. When this training stabilizes, GANs can generate outputs that are often indistinguishable from real data.

Cycle-consistent GANs (CycleGANs) are a widely used extension of GANs for cross-modality image translation, especially when paired datasets (for example, MRI and CT acquired in the same patient, with perfect alignment) are unavailable. Unlike paired image-to-image GANs, Cycle GANs learn 2 mappings between source and target domains (A→B and B→A) and enforce a cycle-consistency constraint, such that translating an image from one domain to the other and back again approximately reconstructs the original input. This constraint is commonly used in medical imaging to reduce the risk that translation “drifts” away from the underlying anatomy, making Cycle-GAN-style models a widely adopted approach for tasks such as MRI↔CT synthesis, artifact suppression and protocol or contrast harmonization. Many medical implementations also incorporate identity and structure-preserving losses such as segmentation – or deformation-aware terms, to further constrain anatomy and mitigate hallucinations beyond basic cycle-consistency.

Recent developments in neural network architecture further illustrate the progressive refinement of GAN-based models to meet the specific technical and anatomical requirements of clinical imaging. These address the limitations in conventional convolutional GANs used such as limited long-range contextual modeling, vulnerability to texture-structure inconsistency and reduced robustness in anatomically complex regions. Wu et al. proposed CTRF-GAN, a hybrid generative framework that unifies transformer-driven attention mechanisms, which help the model understand long-range structural relationships, with Fourier-domain convolution, which helps the model capture fine anatomical detail while preserving sharpness and anatomical plausibility. In practical terms, this would allow a model to correct for metal artifacts, restore motion-blurred or missing slices and enhance resolution, especially applicable to spinal imaging where patient motion and implanted hardware are common.

Historically, medical image translation has relied predominantly on GAN-based models rather than diffusion models for several practical reasons. Many translation pipelines require fast inference with GANs typically generating images in a single forward pass, whereas classical diffusion sampling requires many iterative denoising steps. GAN-based translation also matured much earlier in the literature and tooling ecosystem, leading to broader clinical experimentation and benchmark adoption. Furthermore, in data-constrained settings, as can be common in medical imaging, paired or semipaired GAN objectives alongside CycleGAN’s cycle-consistency constraint in unpaired settings) have often been viewed as pragmatic ways to encourage anatomical fidelity and reduce mode-averaging, particularly when training data are limited or heterogenous. While diffusion models can offer superior training stability and high-fidelity synthesis, their computational cost and the need for careful conditioning limited early clinical deployment, although newer accelerated and constrained diffusion approaches are increasingly narrowing this gap.

Where studies did not explicitly name the translation architecture, we describe them at the level of the reported objective (paired conditional GAN vs. unpaired cycle-consistent translation) to avoid over-claiming a specific model class.
2. Diffusion Models
Diffusion-based methods represent a more recent advance, synthesizing new data by learning to reverse a gradual noising process.

These models have become the dominant architecture in modern generative AI since they are more stable to train than GANs, achieve state-of-the-art quality with fewer artifacts and allow strong controllability through text prompts, conditioning images, masks or structural constraints. As summarized by Webber and Reader, diffusion models have become central to clinical reconstruction tasks, outperforming GANs in stability and diversity. Their precise control over fidelity and texture is particularly valuable in spine MRI and CT, where artifacts and subtle soft-tissue contrast often obscure fine detail.

While diffusion models now underpin much of modern generative imaging, their traditional limitation has been speed: classical diffusion pipelines require hundreds to thousands of iterative denoising steps, making them too slow for clinical workflows, especially in surgical settings. Recent work has begun to address this barrier through model distillation, latent-space optimization and hardware-aware acceleration. Distilled diffusion models compress the full sampling trajectory into as few as 10, or even a single, inference step, preserving fidelity while reducing sampling time by an order of magnitude. Consistency-based models take this further by learning a direct mapping from noise to image, allowing diffusion-level realism at speeds approaching GAN synthesis. These advances, along with practical hardware optimizations, greatly reduce computational burden and move diffusion methods closer to feasibility within picture archiving and communication system (PACS), MRI reconstruction pipelines or even intraoperative workflows. As discussed later, this trend will be central to whether diffusion models transition from research environments to true clinical deployment.
3. VLMs and 3-Dimensional /Hybrid Generative Models

Recent advancements extend beyond image synthesis and pixel-level reconstruction into multimodal reasoning. VLMs such as contrastive language–image pretraining-derived and medical-domain architectures align imaging and textual information, enabling structured radiology reports and automated annotation. Park et al. demonstrated that VLMs can generate structured, patient-friendly spine MRI reports with a hallucination rate as low as 1.12% across more than 2,000 cases. In this study, hallucinations were defined as clinically significant discrepancies relative to the original radiologist report, including the addition, omission or alteration of medically relevant findings with potential implications for patient care.

It is important to highlight that VLMs differ fundamentally from GANs and diffusion models in that they do not primarily generate medical images themselves, instead interpreting visual inputs and generating structured textual or semantic outputs. In clinical workflows, VLMs typically act as a multimodal reasoning layer, extracting meaning from imaging data and linking this information to reports, annotations or clinical decision support. In some integrated pipelines, VLM-derived semantic guidance may then be used to condition downstream generative models (such as diffusion models) for image synthesis or editing.

Alongside reporting, volumetric reconstruction is a major frontier for spine imaging. Transformer-based 3D generative architectures are enabling models to integrate volumetric imaging, cross-sectional context and clinical metadata. Artsi et al. showed how multimodal large language models support spine reporting through enhanced contextual interpretation, while emerging volumetric workflows described by Lin et al. introduce cross-plane generative reasoning with improved anatomical consistency. Such models move beyond traditional 2-dimensional pipelines and support more anatomically grounded, explainable and workflow-integrated applications, which will be discussed later. The principal generative frameworks and their clinical applications are summarized in Fig. 1.
CLINICAL APPLICATIONS

Generative models are now entering clinical imaging pipelines in several distinct ways: through augmentation of training datasets to improve downstream diagnostic models; reconstruction or synthesis of missing sequences at the point of care; cross-modality image generation (e.g., MRI-to-CT) to support planning and radiation reduction; multimodal report generation and workflow automation via VLMs; and 3-dimensional (3D) reconstruction for surgical navigation. Rather than functioning as standalone tools, these systems can be embedded within existing radiology or surgical workflows, generating additional images, annotations or decision-support outputs that clinicians review and verify. The following sections outline how major generative model classes are currently being used in practical clinical settings, with the emphasis being on practical deployment mechanisms and clinical integration rather than performance metrics alone.
1. Training Augmentation Supporting Clinical Deployment

Generative models are increasingly used to address class imbalance within spinal imaging datasets, particularly for underrepresented groups such as infections, tumors, congenital deformities, postoperative changes and rare degenerative variants. By synthesizing anatomically plausible examples of these less common entities, generative augmentation improves model exposure to the full spectrum of spinal pathology, reducing overfitting to common patterns. Although augmentation itself occurs before deployment, these techniques indirectly influence clinical performance by reducing false negatives in rare entities such as infection, tumor or postoperative complications, thereby supporting more reliable decision‑making in routine practice.

A notable clinical demonstration of this comes from Schlaeger et al., who integrated GAN-generated synthetic T2-weighted fat-suppressed sequences into routine lumbar MRI interpretation across a large multicenter cohort. The addition of synthetic images both significantly improved radiologist diagnostic agreement (κ increasing from 0.56 to 0.65) and enhanced accuracy for several less frequent findings, including Modic changes, marrow abnormalities and early infectious processes. Importantly, this did not only just demonstrate measurable diagnostic benefit, but since the GAN was trained on heterogenous data from 38 scanners, suggested that this augmentation could increase robustness even under real-world variability.

Beyond diagnostic interpretation, generative augmentation also improves the performance of downstream segmentation and classification tasks. Han et al. showed that incorporating GAN-derived synthetic examples into vertebral and disc segmentation improved Dice scores, especially in anatomically atypical or degenerated spines, highlighting that synthetic samples could help models better generalize to outlier cases. Similar gains in model sensitivity and recall for uncommon pathology types have been reported in studies of fracture classification and volumetric reconstruction, as well as in intraoperative multimodal synthesis where GAN-generated MRI volumes from ultrasound input enhanced anatomical localization.

More broadly, systematic evaluations of augmentation methods indicate consistent improvements in model generalization, with reported gains across area under the curve (AUC), sensitivity and Dice coefficients when synthetic data are incorporated. While technical challenges such as mode collapse and limited diversity remain active areas of refinement, a recent preprint suggests that architectural advances enabling targeted, pathology-aware augmentation may further improve reliability. These findings support the premise that generative augmentation can be a practical and scalable strategy to address class imbalance in spine imaging, expanding the coverage of underrepresented pathology spaces, complementing its broader use in reconstruction, synthesis and multimodal integration.
2. Image Reconstruction and Enhancement

In clinical workflows, generative reconstruction models are typically deployed as preprocessing tools that generate additional sequences, enhance resolution or reduce noise before radiologist review. These models take existing imaging sequences as input and synthesize higher-quality or missing images that are displayed alongside conventional scans in PACS for verification and interpretation.

Diffusion-based models are increasingly being explored as iterative reconstruction engines within imaging pipelines. In practice, these systems take accelerated, noisy or low-dose acquisitions as input and progressively denoise them to produce higher-quality images before they appear in PACS. For spine imaging, this allows shorter scan times or reduced radiation dose while preserving fine osseous and neural detail needed for stenosis grading and preoperative planning. Although still emerging, diffusion-based reconstruction is being evaluated as a complement or alternative to GAN-based enhancement in scenarios where training stability and anatomical consistency are prioritized.

Early studies have demonstrated the clear clinical utility, in terms of metrics, of GANs. Schlaeger et al. used adversarial learning to generate fat-suppressed T2-weighted lumbar MRI sequences directly from standard T1/T2 sequence inputs. These GAN-generated images cut scan times by around 40% while maintaining radiologist diagnostic confidence comparable to conventional methods. Kudo et al. extended this approach to virtual thin-slice reconstruction showing that GAN-produced 1 mm sagittal slices acted to enhance the visibility of vertebral endplates and improved anatomical numbering accuracy in cases of collapsed-disc and osteophytes. These enhancements support clearer evaluation of disc margins, endplate definition, facet joint detail, and the dimensions of the central and foraminal canals, all of which can assist diagnostic assessment. More advanced GAN variants, such as CTRF-GAN, further have the potential to enhance reconstruction robustness, particularly in cases involving implants or motion artifact, as discussed earlier.

In clinical deployment, multimodal VLMs are most commonly used as reporting assistants embedded within PACS or radiology information systems. These systems ingest imaging data or draft reports and generate structured summaries that radiologists review, edit and approve before final sign-off, improving consistency and reducing reporting burden. Recent work shows that multimodal vision-language systems can generate structured draft reports that radiologists can review and edit, reducing cognitive load and improving consistency across readers. By standardizing recurrent descriptors (e.g., disc degeneration, canal compromise, foraminal narrowing etc.) these models help minimize inter-reader terminology variability, an effect also noted in industry evaluations of generative reporting systems. Early multimodal implementations additionally suggest a potential role in workflow prioritization, with systems capable of flagging potentially urgent findings for expedited review, although such applications still require careful human oversight to ensure reliability. It is these functions which substantially strengthen the case for easing the integration of generative AI into everyday radiology workflows, offering clear practical value.
3. Cross-Modality Synthesis and Radiation Reduction

In current clinical prototypes, cycle-consistent GAN-based models are deployed as image-to-image translators that convert one routinely acquired modality into another within the same spatial frame. For example, a CT scan can be processed to generate a synthetic MRI or vice versa, with the generated series loaded directly into PACS or planning software alongside the source images. This allows clinicians to scroll through co-registered views without additional registration steps, enabling MRIlike soft-tissue contrast when MRI is unavailable or reducing the need for repeat imaging while retaining the original acquisition as the diagnostic reference.

Beyond reconstruction, generative modeling now supports patient-specific simulation. Ahmadian et al. developed a digital‑twin vertebra framework that combines generative reconstruction of patient‑specific vertebrae with finite‑element simulation to model fracture propagation and the biomechanical impact of vertebroplasty, outperforming conventional population‑based models in predicting post‑treatment vertebral stability. In parallel, Lee et al. proposed a lumbar spine CT‑to‑MRI synthesis network based on a cycle‑consistent GAN, producing synthetic T2‑weighted lumbar MR images from CT that were rated by blinded radiologists as closely matching true MRI in anatomical fidelity and tissue contrast, highlighting a potential role as an alternative when MRI is contraindicated or unavailable.

However, while synthetic CT generation provides a promising radiation-free alternative, several studies have highlighted important limitations. In particular, synthetic CT produced from single-sequence or low-resolution MRI inputs may exhibit reduced accuracy in axial or pedicle-level measurements, where even minor geometric deviations are clinically relevant. These discrepancies have been documented in comparative evaluations showing that synthetic CT can underestimate pedicle width or distort local osseous contours, especially when derived from single-sequence or thick-slice MRI, reflecting challenges in MRI-to-CT registration and the lower spatial resolution of conventional MRI. These plane-specific discrepancies underscore the need for volumetric or multisequence MRI acquisitions when generating synthetic CT for tasks such as pedicle screw planning or fine osseous assessment.

The strongest clinical motivation for sCT is in those radiation-sensitive groups (e.g., pediatric patients, pregnant individuals, or those requiring repeated imaging) where reducing cumulative dose is especially important. Recent work demonstrates that substituting sCT for conventional CT in these populations can substantially reduce radiation exposure while maintaining acceptable accuracy for preoperative planning, although mild deviations in pedicle measurements have been noted in the presence of degenerative changes or MRI-related geometric distortion. Studies comparing pedicle screw guides generated from sCT versus CT similarly indicate comparable translational and angular accuracy, but emphasize the importance of high-quality multisequence MRI input to mitigate these residual limitations.
4. Segmentation and Deformity Assessment

Generative approaches also enhance segmentation performance by enforcing anatomical coherence across slices, an advantage over purely discriminative architectures. By learning global shape priors, these models help maintain vertebral alignment, disc boundaries and canal margins even in cases where image quality is poor or anatomy is distorted. For example, Han et al. showed that incorporating a generative component improved cross-slice vertebral continuity and disc/canal boundary definition on MRI, particularly in atypical or degenerated spines. This aligns with earlier work demonstrating that global shape constraints can preserve vertebral consistency in multi-slice segmentation tasks, valuable in applications such as implant sizing, alignment planning and deformity correction.

Kumar et al. extended these principles by introducing a hybrid framework which combined GANs with spectral clustering (a machine learning technique which groups data based on relationships or similarity), that automates cervical spine segmentation and deformity diagnosis. This framework, when applied to 3 medical imaging datasets, achieved +8.3% accuracy, +8.5% recall and a 1.9% reduction in diagnostic time relative to conventional pipelines. Complementary multimodal systems such as SpinalSAM-R1, which combines an enhanced Segment Anything-based vision model with the DeepSeek-R1 large language model, represent a further evolution of this trend, allowing users to perform natural-language-guided lumbar CT segmentation, rather than relying solely on manual or graphical inputs. Using a dataset of 120 lumbar CT scans (>30,000 slices), this system achieved state-of-the-art segmentation performances, outperforming established models, correctly interpreting 94.3% of user commands and producing results in under 800 msec. Thus, these developments signal a shift from segmentation as a purely technical step to a clinically actionable process, where generative shape modeling allows downstream tasks to become more standardized, reproducible and less dependent on operator expertise.
5. Surgical Planning and Intraoperative Navigation

For surgical planning, generative models are typically integrated as reconstruction engines that convert incomplete or low-quality imaging inputs into volumetric anatomical models suitable for measurement and trajectory planning. These generated volumes are reviewed alongside source imaging within planning or navigation platforms before clinical use.

Beyond VLM-driven systems, emerging 3D generative and reconstruction models are reshaping spine imaging pipelines. Models such as ReVerteR are able to generate full 3D reconstructions of the vertebral column from 3D biplanar radiographs or incomplete CT data, recovering spatial geometry with enough fidelity for surgical planning and intraoperative localization. Similarly, Hu et al. demonstrated an ultrasound-based AI systems that employs volumetric methods with the overarching aim of achieving radiation-free intraoperative navigation. The authors developed a standalone ultrasound-guided navigation system, which reconstructs highly visualized 3D spine volumes using a deep-learning network capable of reliably extracting bone surfaces from tracked ultrasound sweeps. Their system achieved high geometric accuracy (with a mean absolute error of 1.19±0.85 mm), showed robust generalization across patients with atypical anatomy, and reduced workflow time to under 8 minutes compared with around 20 minutes for CT-based workflows. These reconstructions could have the potential to support several other spine-specific tasks, including anticipating screw breach risk, assessing suitability for cortical-trajectory implants, planning osteotomies, and assisting with dependable intraoperative level identification. Together, these systems point toward a future in which preoperative planning and intraoperative guidance become increasingly radiation-free, real-time, and patient-specific, with generative models serving as the connective layer between low-quality inputs (e.g., ultrasound) and decision-grade 3D anatomical reconstructions. The authors did not, however, report a dedicated analysis of phantom artifacts or structural hallucinations in the reconstructed volumes; potential spurious or missing bony features were only indirectly captured through these distance‑based metrics. This supports the geometric feasibility of ultrasound‑based navigation but also underscores the need for future work that explicitly audits hallucinated structures, especially in severe deformity or revision cases.

Taken together, these developments show that generative models are moving beyond experimental image synthesis toward workflow-embedded clinical tools that support reconstruction, reporting, planning and navigation across the spine imaging pathway. Although translation into routine care remains early, the convergence of these applications suggests a shift toward multimodal, patient-specific and clinically integrated spine imaging systems. The spectrum of clinical applications, from data augmentation through surgical planning, is illustrated in Fig. 2.
CHALLENGES AND LIMITATIONS

Before discussing the ongoing and future challenges in utilizing generative AI in this clinical context, we feel it is important to highlight that as a narrative review (rather than a systematic review), study inclusion and emphasis were partly judgment-based, and the synthesis may therefore be subject to selection bias despite multidatabase searching and citation chasing. Potential sources include language restriction, database choice, and rapidly evolving terminology within the generative AI literature. While we attempted to minimize this through utilizing SANRA principles, there was still judgment involved in our selection and synthesis of the final papers.
1. External Validation and Generalizability

Despite impressive accuracy in research settings, AI models, including state-of-the-art generative systems, often lack robust external validation. Ali et al. emphasized that even high-performing discriminative models can fail when applied to new patient cohorts or imaging protocols. For example, Voter et al. reported poor diagnostic accuracy for AI tools in cervical spine fracture detection in real-world test cohorts, with sensitivity plummeting to 54.9% compared to expected performance. Failure-mode analysis showed that many missed fractures were chronic, subtle vertebral-body or osteophyte injuries likely underrepresented in the original training data, underscoring how external performance can collapse on rare or atypical patterns due to unbalanced training sets. Park et al. echoed this, noting that hallucinations, bias and performance drift are persistent, requiring domain-specific validation standards for safe use in radiology.

Concrete examples in the spine domain illustrate these challenges. A 2024 study using GAN-generated synthetic spine radiographs, including a domain-specific “SpineGAN”, showed that while models trained on synthetic data performed well in abnormality detection (AUC 0.830 vs. 0.856 for real data), subtle and rare pathologies were underrepresented, resulting in reduced diagnostic confidence and overfitting to common variants. Similarly, a recent preprint suggests that diffusion models trained to generate cervical spine x-rays produced visually convincing images rated as realistic by radiologists, yet still required manual exclusion of anatomically implausible cases, demonstrating the ongoing need for expert oversight to filter synthetic hallucinations.
2. Hallucinations and Anatomical Inaccuracies
1) General anatomical hallucination risk and safeguards

Similarly, over-reliance on synthetic data for training introduces domain drift, where model errors accumulate across retraining cycles, gradually diverging from genuine anatomy, particularly in complex conditions such as scoliosis, postoperative spines or congenital deformities. The clinical consequences can be significant in spinal imaging, where even a minor hallucination, such as an inaccurate pedicle contour or an omitted foraminal stenosis, could misinform implant sizing or surgical level selection. Morbée et al. demonstrated statistical equivalency between deep-learning-derived synthetic CT and conventional CT for geometrical measurements of lumbar vertebrae, including pedicle width, supporting the feasibility of synthetic imaging for surgical planning. While measurement differences fell within equivalence margins, the authors and related literature highlight that in clinical navigation and pedicle screw placement, even small measurement errors (as little as 2 mm) can be significant, leading to neurovascular injury or pedicle breach risk, underscoring the need for continued validation, especially in anatomically challenging or high-risk scenarios.

Several strategies have been proposed and utilized to reduce structurally implausible outputs and mitigate the risk of anatomically significant hallucinations in synthetic spine imaging. Atlas- or template-based vertebral shape priors can be incorporated to constrain generated volumes within anatomically plausible cortical and pedicle boundaries, using population-derived 3D vertebral meshes or signed-distance-field representations. Segmentation-guided or multitask generative frameworks, in which models jointly synthesize imaging and predict vertebral or pedicle segmentations, may further enforce structural fidelity through shared feature representations and geometric loss functions. Additional approaches include topology-preserving surface or distance-based losses that penalize deviations in vertebral contours, as well as morphometric constraint priors that restrict predictions to biologically plausible ranges for pedicle width, height and vertebral geometry unless strongly supported by input data. Shape-aware discriminators or auxiliary critics that evaluate global vertebral morphology rather than local intensity patches alone have also been proposed to improve anatomical consistency. Collectively, these structurally constrained or hybrid generative–segmentation pipelines represent an important direction for reducing clinically consequential hallucinations in high-precision applications such as pedicle screw planning.
2) Model-specific hallucination mechanisms across generative paradigms

Hallucination manifests differently across major generative model families and therefore requires model-specific safeguards. For GAN-based systems, adversarial training instabilities and mode collapse can lead to anatomically implausible structures that nonetheless appear realistic at the patch level, particularly when rare deformities, postoperative constructs or unstable fractures are underrepresented in the training data. This may result in “filled-in” canals, smoothed pedicles or omission of hardware while still achieving favorable visual similarity metrics such as Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) or Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM).

In diffusion models, iterative denoising generally preserves global structure more reliably but subtle boundary shifts in cortical bone, endplates or neural foramina may accumulate across sampling steps, producing small yet clinically important geometric deviations that are not captured by conventional pixel-wise metrics. Such deviations may be particularly relevant in measurement-dependent tasks such as pedicle sizing or deformity assessment.

For VLMs, hallucinations predominantly arise at the semantic or report level rather than in pixel-level image synthesis. VLMs may generate plausible but incorrect descriptions of vertebral level, laterality or severity, introduce nonexistent findings, or omit critical qualifiers even when underlying imaging features are correctly localized. Recent work in spine MRI reporting demonstrates low but nonzero rates of clinically significant “artificial hallucinations” in patient-facing summaries derived from radiology reports.

Recognizing these distinct failure modes is essential for designing targeted mitigation strategies. These may include anatomy- aware discriminators, segmentation-conditioned training and shape-constrained losses for GAN-based models; uncertainty-aware visualization and cross-modality verification for diffusion-based synthesis; and strict image-text consistency checks, structured reporting templates and human-in-the-loop validation for VLM-generated outputs. Explicitly distinguishing these model-specific risks reinforces that hallucination in generative spine imaging is not a single phenomenon but a spectrum of structural, probabilistic and semantic failure modes requiring tailored safeguards.

In addition to architectural safeguards, rigorous dataset curation to oversample rare anatomies, adversarial stress‑testing on edge‑case scenarios, and tight scoping of model prompts and outputs will be essential to keep these diverse hallucination modes within acceptable clinical bounds.
3. Bias, Imbalance, and Population Representativeness

Beyond anatomical fidelity, bias amplification represents another critical limitation. Khosravi et al. demonstrated in pelvic radiograph synthesis that even visually indistinguishable GAN-generated images can magnify demographic or disease biases embedded in the source data. When the original corpus contained an overrepresentation of certain demographic groups, the GAN reproduced and even exaggerated those imbalances. Classifiers trained on such synthetic data performed well on majority groups but misclassified underrepresented cases, a phenomenon equally concerning for spine imaging where demographic skew and uneven pathology prevalence are common. This finding underscores that synthetic realism does not guarantee representativeness and that uncorrected generative pipelines may reinforce existing inequities across sex, age or racial subgroups. Within spine imaging, this could manifest as poorer performance in recognizing degenerative patterns common in specific ethnic groups, underrepresentation of postoperative hardware configurations, or reduced accuracy in scoliosis subtypes that are rare in training data.

This challenge is only amplified in generative AI, where synthetic data and reconstructions can often overlook rare pathologies, introducing risks of clinically relevant hallucinations or bias, unless rigorously checked against real-world evidence. As noted by several reviews, synthetic datasets often fail to capture nuanced (spinal) morphology or rare phenotypes, reinforcing the need for comprehensive bias auditing and demographic balancing.

A 2023 study training convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on synthetic spine radiographs found that while classifiers achieved high accuracy overall, detection of rare findings like spondylolysis or hardware complications was noticeably poorer in models exposed only to synthetic data, underscoring the risk that key features are underrepresented or missed entirely in generative samples. In addition, several systematic and technical reviews discuss how AI models, including generative ones, may demonstrate instability when applied across different MRI vendors, sequences, or institutions, unless specifically calibrated or retrained. While direct, public evidence for vendor-specific generative instability in spinal imaging is currently limited, analogous effects are well-established in other musculoskeletal imaging domains, and underscore the need for rigorous cross-vendor validation and standardization in AI model development.

Thus, while these studies show that generative AI enhances image realism and privacy, rigorous real-world validation and continuous oversight are essential to prevent clinically significant hallucinations and ensure safe, equitable deployment in spinal imaging.
4. Dataset Quality, Scale, and Standardization

Spine imaging datasets remain small, unbalanced and heterogenous, hindering model generalization and limiting the success of even the most advanced AI algorithms. Many existing public datasets contain fewer than 1,000 images and are fragmented across institutions, resulting in inadequate representation of rare spine pathologies and demographic diversity. This fragmentation is made worse by the pronounced variability in acquisition protocols with a range of differences including MRI field strength, sequence choice, coil configuration, reconstruction algorithms and slice thickness, all further impeding reproducibility and cross-center studies.

Compared to large-scale repositories in other imaging domains, such as CheXpert for chest x-rays or ImageNet for natural images, the spine field lacks robust standardized public benchmarks, making it difficult for investigators to rigorously compare and validate new algorithms. Even the largest open-access, well-annotated spine datasets such as Cervical Spine x-ray Atlas remain limited by their size (under 5,000 cases) and by their focus on specific regions. Since synthetic data augmentation efforts rely on this limited real image diversity for training, this contributes to the failure to represent the full spectrum of spinal pathologies or implant types.

Coupled with a lack of universal enforcement of annotation quality and consistency (such as standardization of label definitions, measurement protocols and multiexpert validation), this further restricts the external validity of any models developed. Collectively, these gaps mean that spine imaging AI research needs to confront major obstacles in dataset size, standardization and diversity, with these issues having to be addressed through broader data sharing, multi-institutional collaboration and creation of high-quality, annotated repositories if the field is to achieve reliable, generalizable AI solutions.
5. Evaluation and Benchmarking

A related barrier lies in the absence of standardized evaluation frameworks for generative and discriminative models in spine imaging. Most studies rely on technical metrics such as the SSIM, FID, accuracy or mean average precision, but these metrics whilst capturing visual realism or prediction precision do not necessarily reflect clinical diagnostic reliability. Radiologist-blinded evaluations and outcome-based validations remain rare, often leading to inflated perceptions of model maturity and readiness for deployment.

Recent studies have stressed that although technical performance, reflected by high accuracy or AUC scores, may approach expert levels, genuine clinical utility depends on reproducibility and cross-institutional robustness. For example, a CNN for lumbar disc grading achieved a precision of 0.75 and recall of 0.81, yet performance dropped substantially in rare or severely degenerated discs due to inconsistent annotation and poor image quality. Evaluation standards that rely solely on such technical metrics risk overestimating clinical readiness. Without rigorous multicenter trials, prospective validation, and outcome-linked benchmarks, the true translational impact of AI, generative or otherwise, remains uncertain.
6. Operational, Technical, and Ethical Challenges
1) Technical fragility, computational constraints, and infrastructure limitations

Methodological and dataset limitations are only part of the picture; workflow and system-level barriers also play a major role in restricting clinical deployment. Despite major advances in model architecture, generative systems such as GANs and diffusion models remain technically fragile and computationally demanding in medical imaging applications. Training instability can manifest as mode collapse, gradient explosion or nonconvergence, limiting reproducibility and scalability across datasets. As highlighted by Shiri et al., small perturbations in hyperparameters, learning rate or input balance can cause entire networks to fail or generate unrealistic datasets, especially prominent in musculoskeletal datasets, where limited sample diversity and high anatomical complexity make convergence difficult to achieve.

Recent innovations, including conditional random field-enhanced GAN architecture, have attempted to address these challenges by improving memory efficiency and stabilising high-resolution 3D training. Early results in non-spinal datasets, such as lung and brain MRI, suggest improved recall and F1 scores for nodule detection. While these models produce superior textural realism and geometric consistency, their performance depends heavily on access to large-paired datasets and high-end computational hardware, resources that remain concentrated in specialised research institutions. In practice, this technical fragility means that reproducing published results across clinical centres is often infeasible without extensive re-engineering, calibration and infrastructure investment.
2) Clinician trust, interpretability, and uncertainty

Beyond technical issues, the success of any tool in a surgical setting relies on clinician trust and literacy. These models largely remain “black-boxes,” offering little explanation of how or why a reconstruction was produced. This opacity undermines confidence among radiologists and surgeons, who may overtrust high-fidelity synthetic images or disregard them entirely due to lack of understanding, overconfidence in realistic-looking synthetic data potentially masking subtle anatomical inaccuracies, especially when technical benchmarks are outpacing clinical validation.

Clinician educational preparedness and understanding is another critical bottleneck, with few recent discussions centred around how few clinicians receive formal training in AI interpretation or bias detection. Similarly, interpretability also impedes clinician confidence, with many commercial or research prototypes providing little, if any, information about uncertainty levels or model confidence. This leaves a limited ability to audit, correct or override algorithmic results, eroding clinical accountability. Awasthi et al. demonstrated that visualizing AI-detected errors and providing interactive correction tools substantially improves diagnostic performance and safety, however, these functionalities are still rare outside research settings. Only with a combination of dedicated training, transparent interfaces and interface validation, can we be assured that clinicians will safely adopt technologies.
3) Ethical, legal, and regulatory challenges

Finally, there remain complex ethical and legal questions surrounding data provenance, consent and accountability, with synthetic image generation blurring the boundary between authentic and artificial patient data. As Templin et al. and Wang et al. describe, when models generate anatomically plausible yet fabricated content, provenance tracking becomes nearly impossible, raising uncertainty over who is legally responsible should such images inform clinical decisions. Indeed, recent reviews caution that advanced generative models may unintentionally leak patient identity features despite anonymisation, raising new privacy and re-identification risks which current policies fail to address.

On the other side, regulatory agencies are facing additional challenges trying to keep these in-check. Traditional U.S. Food and Drug Administration/European Medicines Agency frameworks are built around “locked” algorithms which do not change post-approval. Generative/iterative systems, by definition, continuously retrain and adapt to new data, creating a moving target for oversight. With clinical concerns such as hallucinated or nonexistent structures, especially in diagnostic or preoperative contexts, posing significant medicolegal risks, as shown in studies of synthetic spine CTs and MRIs where phantom artifacts occasionally appeared, it is clear this needs to be quickly addressed. The key barriers to clinical translation, including validation gaps, anatomical inaccuracies, bias amplification, and regulatory uncertainty, are summarized in Fig. 3.
FUTURE DIRECTIONS AND CLINICAL IMPLICATIONS

These limitations collectively define the roadmap for the next phase of development. Despite rapid progress, generative AI in spine imaging is still at an early stage, and the path to reliable clinical deployment depends on deliberate, coordinated development across data, modeling and evaluation. Several priorities emerge from the limitations outlined above. These coordinated priorities for clinical deployment are outlined in Fig. 4.

First, improvements in data quality and standardization remain essential. For the spine, establishing harmonized annotation frameworks, including standardized Pfirrmann disc grades, Modic endplate categories, canal and foraminal stenosis scales, vertebral numbering conventions (especially in transitional anatomy), and deformity parameters, will be essential for building reproducible training corpora and ensuring cross-vendor interoperability. Current spine datasets are small, unevenly distributed and inconsistently annotated, which limits generalizability and fuels the very instability that generative systems are prone to. Multi-institutional collaboration to create larger, diverse and consistently labelled datasets will be needed, together with structured benchmarks that allow fair comparison of model performance. Work by Haddad et al. and Sigurdarson et al. already emphasizes the importance of public datasets and shared quality assurance frameworks for adult spinal deformity and related applications, reinforcing that broader data harmonization is foundational.

A major practical step will be the creation of multicenter, cross-vendor spine imaging benchmarks. Unlike other imaging domains, the spine field lacks standardized tasks, shared annotations and open leaderboards. A coordinated effort to create high-quality, consistently labelled datasets spanning lumbar, cervical and thoracic imaging, including rare entities such as postoperative changes, infection and congenital deformity, would provide the foundation needed for fair and reproducible model evaluation. Crucially, these repositories should include harmonized labeling systems (e.g., standardized Modic, Pfirrmann, and stenosis grading) and cross-vendor MRI/CT acquisition profiles to ensure that generative systems are not inadvertently tuned to narrow or idiosyncratic data distributions.

A second priority is the development of federated and privacy-preserving generative models. Given that spinal imaging data cannot easily be pooled due to privacy constraints, federated generative frameworks, such as those described by Wang et al. and Guan et al., may allow distributed training across institutions while maintaining patient confidentiality. This would improve representation of rare pathologies and reduce the vendor-specific biases that currently limit external validity.

Generative AI is also likely to move toward deeper multimodal integration. As highlighted by Azad et al. and Franceschini et al., combining imaging with electronic health records, surgical metadata and biomechanical or finite-element modeling can support more context-aware generative systems. These hybrid frameworks could underpin digital twin pipelines for deformity progression modeling, outcome prediction or personalised surgical planning, offering a more comprehensive and clinically relevant understanding of each patient.

Beyond single-paradigm models, generative AI is likely to move towards hybrid architectures which integrate complementary mechanisms. Recent work in medical imaging has begun to compare GANs, transformers and diffusion models, with emerging consensus that practical clinical systems will increasingly combine these paradigms to balance fidelity, diversity, controllability and computational efficiency. In spine imaging, GAN-diffusion hybrids could couple the sharpness and fast inference of GAN-based synthesis with the short denoising and reconstruction capabilities of diffusion models, while transformer-augmented generators can improve long-range anatomical consistency across multi-level deformity, postoperative constructs and longitudinal follow-up. Generative–discriminative hybrid frameworks, such as MedSymmFlow, further illustrate this trajectory by integrating image synthesis, classification and calibrated uncertainty estimation within a unified architecture, enabling digital-twin–style pipelines that support deformity modeling, surgical planning and risk estimation. These hybrid generative systems are likely to underpin clinically deployable spine imaging AI, but their safe implementation will still depend on the same prerequisites outlined in our roadmap: harmonized data standards, federated training frameworks, outcome-linked validation and tight integration into routine clinical workflow.

Alongside these modeling advances, transparent interpretability, explainability and uncertainty quantification must become standard features of future clinically deployable systems. For spine surgery applications, uncertainty must be communicated in a spatially intuitive and decision-relevant manner. Voxelwise confidence heatmaps overlaid on reconstructed anatomy and planned screw trajectories represent the most immediately translatable approach, as they can be integrated into existing navigation interfaces and computed in real time from ensemble or epistemic uncertainty estimates. Such overlays allow surgeons to identify regions where synthetic CT or reconstructed anatomy may be less reliable, especially around pedicle cortices, vertebral endplates or the spinal canal, without disrupting familiar workflow. Emerging work in surgical navigation suggests that more advanced trajectory-centric visualizations may further enhance intraoperative usability, including tubular uncertainty bands along planned screw paths, compact tool-mounted indicators that combine deviation and model confidence, and region-specific alerts that flag high-uncertainty synthetic structures for manual verification against source imaging.

Studies such as Karthik et al. demonstrate that uncertainty overlays can reveal epistemic gaps in MR-to-CT synthesis, especially in atypical or scoliotic spines, improving clinician trust and diagnostic safety. For example, confidence heatmaps overlaying planned screw trajectories can highlight areas where bone boundary segmentation is uncertain. Integrating these tools into next-generation generative–discriminative hybrids, such as MedSymmFlow, may provide a practical foundation for safe clinical deployment, enabling clinicians to audit synthetic features in real time while preserving workflow efficiency and decision accountability. We therefore prioritise confidence heatmaps as an immediately translatable solution, while recognizing that more advanced trajectory‑aware interfaces are likely to become feasible as real‑time generative and uncertainty‑aware navigation systems mature.

Evaluation frameworks will also need to evolve. Future studies should prioritize radiologist-blinded assessment, external validation across multiple institutions and outcome-linked metrics, such as reoperation rates and functional outcomes, rather than relying solely on technical measures like SSIM or FID. As recent work has shown, high technical scores often mask clinically important errors, and only rigorous cross-centre testing can provide decision-grade confidence. Prospective, registrylinked evaluation platforms, similar to those suggested by Sigurdarson et al., may offer a path to systematic, real-world monitoring.

Equally important will be genuine workflow integration. For generative models to move beyond research prototypes, they will need to operate seamlessly within existing PACS environments, support DICOM-native outputs and allow radiologists to view, edit and, where necessary, reverse synthetic reconstructions. Features such as uncertainty overlays, version tracking, and explicit provenance tags identifying synthetic content will be particularly important for clinician trust and regulatory compliance. Embedding these capabilities within routine workflow, rather than in parallel software, will determine whether generative systems are adopted or remain siloed in specialist research settings.

Finally, computational accessibility will be central to clinical translation. The evolution of lighter, more efficient generative architectures, including hybrid transformer-GAN frameworks and optimised diffusion pipelines, may allow real-time use within PACS, intraoperative navigation and radiation-free imaging workflows. As Langlotz et al. note, reducing computing requirements and simplifying model deployment will be essential for moving these systems beyond specialist research centres.
CONCLUSION

Generative AI demonstrates transformative potential in spine imaging, achieving measurable improvements: 40% reduction in MRI scan times while maintaining diagnostic fidelity, <1.12% hallucination rates in report generation, 8.3% segmentation accuracy gains, and 25% enhanced surgical planning precision through digital twin models. However, critical barriers impede clinical translation. External validation reveals performance collapse in real-world cohorts (cervical fracture detection sensitivity dropping to 54.9%), with anatomical hallucinations risking surgical misguidance. In the spine, such failures may translate to missed cervical fractures, mischaracterisation of unstable burst fractures, or inaccurate vertebral level identification, all of which carry substantial neurological and medicolegal consequences. Bias amplification perpetuates demographic underrepresentation from limited, fragmented datasets (<1,000 images) lacking standardized benchmarks. Current evaluation metrics (SSIM, FID, AUC) demonstrate poor correlation with diagnostic reliability, while technical fragility manifests as mode collapse and vendor-specific instability. Operational obstacles include DICOM integration failures and regulatory uncertainty for continuously-learning systems.

Clinical deployment necessitates: (1) federated learning frameworks aggregating multi-institutional, cross-vendor datasets; (2) uncertainty quantification enabling clinician oversight; (3) prospective outcome-linked validation replacing technical metrics; (4) workflow-native systems with provenance tracking; and (5) regulatory pathways for adaptive models. In spine imaging, these advances directly influence clinical care by supporting more reliable stenosis grading, improving the accuracy of preoperative pedicle and alignment measurements, reducing wrong-level surgical risk, and enabling radiation-reduced workflows for vulnerable populations. Rigorous, clinically grounded validation is essential to transition GenAI from research novelty to foundational spine imaging technology, with the spine, given its inherent anatomical complexity and traditionally high interpretative variability, standing to gain significantly from these developments.

passages.text: A Roadmap for foundational research on artificial intelligence in medical imaging: from the 2018 NIH/RSNA/ACR/The Academy Workshop

Three-panel comparison of generative AI frameworks (GANs, Diffusion Models, VLMs) with visual mechanism diagrams and key statistics: ~40% MRI scan time reduction, ~25% preoperative planning accuracy, <1.12% hallucination rate. AI, artificial intelligence; GAN, generative adversarial network; MRI, magnetic resonance imaging; VLM, vision-language model.

Five-step clinical pipeline from Data Augmentation→Reconstruction→Cross-Modality→Segmentation→Surgical Planning with quantitative outcomes (κ improvement, scan time reduction, accuracy gains, geometric precision).

Barriers to clinical translation. Summary of key challenges: validation gap (sensitivity drops from 95.0% to 54.9% in external cohorts), anatomical errors (2-mm pedicle breach threshold), bias amplification, dataset scarcity (<1 K vs. 200 K+ images), computational burden (100–1,000 × inference steps), and regulatory uncertainty. The 54.9% sensitivity drop and 2-mm threshold represent critical benchmarks for safe surgical planning. AI, artificial intelligence.

Vertical roadmap with 5 priorities connected by gradient arrow: Multi-Institutional Datasets, Federated Learning, Uncertainty Quantification, Outcome-Linked Validation, Workflow Integration. PACS, picture archiving and communication system.

Literature searchPubMed/MEDLINE, Scopus, Embase, Cochrane Library; supplemented by manual citation searches
Inclusion criteriaFull-text articles published in English from inception to date
Various study designs, including systematic reviews and meta-analyses, descriptive, case-control, cohort, observational, and randomized controlled trials
Studies applying generative AI to spine imaging, or in a radiological context which could be applied to spine
Exclusion criteriaStudies that do not report outcomes
Case reports are excluded
Standalone abstracts and unpublished studies or preprints

Additional searchA manual search was conducted to include references from recently published reviews in spinal imaging and medical generative modeling
Sample size requirementNo strict sample size requirement was applied

Architectures such as CTRF-GAN incorporate transformer attention and Fourier-domain convolution to address limitations of conventional GANs in anatomically complex regions.
